query: Read & listen to native Japanese and Chinese, tap any word alternative

results.title: I tried every language app... Here are the best ones (Tier List)
results.content:
with literally no context? But it is useful for people studying for the HSK exams,
so I would put this in B tier. The Japanese alternative is kanji study. Okay, this app actually surprised me. At first glance, you have the normal decks
from elementary all the way to advanced. I thought that was it. Like all of the other apps, it would just go through the vocabulary
without any context. But when I investigated a bit more,
they actually include graded reader sets. So you can pick up on kanji
as you read the stories. My Japanese level is so beginner
and reading is a pain!1! Sometimes kanji can be pronounced
differently depending on where it is in a sentence, they call it kun yomi and on yomi where on yomi [...] like a mini Netflix. Like FluentU,
you can rewind every sentence, practice your pronunciation,
and get tested with a few games. However, the content library compared to
Netflix is rather small. They are expanding it,
so I'm very looking forward to that. But I must say, the Japanese content
slaps. Currently I'm watching the Frieren chibi
anime on LingoPie, and because I just have my waifu, I'm
going to have to put it in a high A Tier. Okay, I'm not biased. The next app is a pretty small
app actually, and when I was looking for it
on the Play Store, it had zero reviews. With zero reviews, of course
I was curious. So Langtern is an app specifically for Chinese learners
who want to learn native content. The UI is not the best though,
and it seems like they have a lot of features, but [...] a very good quality app, So I’ll put it in A Tier. Okay, so the app that is better
for Japanese readers is Satori Reader. It was recommended on my discord
and I am obsessed. At first glance the UI is kind of weird,
but it works similarly to LingQ. Basically you get a transcript with audio
you can read along with, but where really stands out
is the explanations. I've been listening to the YuYu
Nihongo podcast, and sometimes he would say certain things that people only
with cultural knowledge would understand. But the editors of Satori Reader
takes the time to explain it to us. While other apps might just give us like a
AI generated response. And of course there is a review section. So people who like spaced repetition,
they got you. Honestly, I wish there was more content,

results.title: The 6 Best Apps for Reading Chinese - Mandarin Companion
results.content:
In addition, there are a number of mini-series (a few for each level), which are about 4+ lesson-length chapters that tell a longer story.

### Key features

Every Chinese word in the text can be tapped on for a detailed dictionary definition (including audio). All the characters can also be colored-coded based on their HSK level or set to show pinyin.

Each chapter has a professional recording by a native speaker which highlights each sentence as it’s read. If you are unsure of the meaning of a sentence, it can be tapped on for an English translation which has been done by a human, not Google translate. [...] ### Pricing

Readibu’s free core version includes: a pop-up dictionary, audio word pronunciation, tap tracking, content difficulty analysis, Simplified-Traditional conversion, and Pinyin/Zhuyin support.

The five-dollar monthly subscription unlocks sentence translation, proper noun underlining, a pinyin/definition pop-up quiz, offline reading, and image visualizations for words.

### Evaluation

For intermediate and above learners who can already understand basic native content, Readibu will work great. Unless you’re a very dedicated reader or tackling a difficult text, the free version should suffice. But if you’re a lower level reader, most available books will be too difficult and you should consider the other apps on this list. [...] The Optical Character Recognizer (OCR) (paid) optically recognizes Chinese characters in a picture, live on your camera, or currently on your screen (Android only).

### Key features

All three feature a pop-up dictionary that integrates with the main dictionary. By tapping on a word or character on the screen, you are given a popup window showing its pinyin, tone, and definition, with the option to add it to your flashcards. With a further tap, you can view its components, stroke order, and sample sentences.

results.title: 15 Great Free Apps for Studying Japanese
results.content:
## 1. LingoDeer

Great Free Apps for Studying Japanese

LingoDeer will have you speak Japanese and raise your fluency level from day one. It follows a fun building-block approach that feels more like a game. Each lesson applies grammar and vocabulary that you learned from the previous one using several methods of testing.  

Unlike other apps that require you to memorize Japanese vocabulary and phrases without context, LingoDeer features audio from native Japanese speakers and integrates words, sentences and culture naturally so that you can use them in real life. Other awesome features include the ability to turn on furigana (phonetic notations) so you can study kanji and learn the meaning and context of a particle with a simple tap. [...] Great Free Apps to Study Japanese

Japanese by Renzo Inc. takes first prize for design. It features a beautiful, clear interface and understands how a user thinks. It contains an audio clip for all entries and has the most precise layout; for example, with hiragana above the kanji used, each kanji is displayed underneath with its meaning. The app also features a built-in study system, but it’s not as good as Anki.

## 13. Shinobi: Read Japanese Stories

Shinobi Japanese takes a unique approach by teaching through illustrated short stories designed for learners. As you read, you’ll encounter new grammar, kanji, and vocabulary in a natural context, with tap-to-translate features and grammar popups that explain tricky parts as you go. [...] I’m looking for an app with pre-made reading materials using high frequency Japanese, similar to the readings on the J proficiency test. Any ideas?

Vocabies app is a new but really cool. it supports Japanese language I mean pronunciation to listen to. and You can create lists of worrds with kanji too to practice both: meanings and characters.

Go to Google Play Store and type vocabies it will be there:)

results.title: Best Apps to Learn Chinese in 2026: An Honest Guide for Serious Learners
results.content:
For graded readers—stories written for learners at various levels—Du Chinese is excellent. Once you’re past the beginner stage and want to read actual content without drowning in dictionary lookups, graded readers help bridge the gap. The app shows pinyin on tap, tracks your vocabulary, and has enough content that you won’t run out quickly. Du Chinese supports the development of Chinese reading skills and Chinese listening through audio-supported stories, allowing you to improve comprehension by listening to native speakers while following along with the text. The app also incorporates Chinese cultural insights within its stories, giving learners valuable context about traditions and customs. Practice exercises and context-rich activities are included to reinforce learning vocabulary and [...] The spaced repetition is built into the audio, forcing you to recall phrases at increasingly longer intervals. It’s particularly good for tones because you’re constantly listening and repeating with native speakers. Pimsleur also provides focused pronunciation practice, helping learners develop authentic pronunciation through repeated exposure to native speaker models. This approach supports Chinese speaking practice and is effective for improving conversational skills.

Modern alternative: Speechling offers a similar approach with more flexibility, and native-speaker feedback on your recordings. [...] Characters vs. pinyin is a real decision. Pinyin (the romanization system) is like training wheels. Useful at first, but apps that let you coast on pinyin forever are setting you up for a wall later. You’ll “know” 500 words but be unable to read a restaurant menu.

Reading, listening, and speaking develop separately. More than in European languages, these are almost independent skills in Chinese. Many apps focus heavily on reading, leaving learners shocked when they can’t understand native speakers talking at normal speed. To improve Chinese listening, it’s important to practice comprehension with authentic audio content, such as Chinese dramas, TV shows, or videos with subtitles.

results.title: Guide to Learning Japanese: Listening, Speaking, Reading & Writing - HelloTalk Blog | HelloTalk
results.content:
Suitable for: Intermediate learners who want to train listening and reading simultaneously through authentic materials, especially those who have mastered basic grammar and kana.

#### LingoDeer

LingoDeer is a language learning app focusing on Asian languages (including Japanese, Korean, and Chinese). The platform offers two complete Japanese courses (Japanese 1 & 2), covering N5 to N3 level content, with short lesson format of approximately 5 minutes each, perfect for daily learning. [...] The platform's core feature is customizable reading experience: users can choose to display kanji, kana only, or kanji with furigana annotations, and can even automatically adjust display based on their kanji knowledge (integrates with WaniKani). Each sentence is accompanied by high-quality audio narrated by native speakers (not machine voice), with single sentence or full article playback options, perfect for shadowing practice. [...] #### Duolingo

Duolingo's Japanese course uses gamified design to help learners build study habits through short, focused exercises. The course covers beginner to intermediate content, including 1,350 kanji (covering JLPT N5, N4, and part of N3).

The platform provides interactive hiragana, katakana, and kanji learning tools, including tracing and writing practice. All sentences are recorded by native speakers (both male and female voices). Duolingo's signature features include daily goal setting, streak tracking, league leaderboards, and other gamification elements that effectively maintain learning motivation. The course also includes a "Stories" feature that provides interesting short reading and listening exercises.
